q: The velocity of a small ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, when dropped in a container filled with glycerine, becomes constant after some time. If the density of glycerine is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, then the viscous force acting on the ball, will be
question: The velocity of a small ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>, when dropped in a container filled with glycerine, becomes constant after some time. If the density of glycerine is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>d</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, then the viscous force acting on the ball, will be
solution:
At the time of terminal velocity ball is in equilibrium i.e. Fg=Fb+Fv

⇒Fv=Fg-Fb

⇒Fv=mg-d2md1g

⇒mg1-d2d1

subject: Physics
q: If an air bubble of diameter $2$ mm rises steadily through a liquid of density $2000$ kg/m$^3$ at a rate of $0.5$ cm/s, then the coefficient of viscosity of liquid is _______ Poise. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question: If an air bubble of diameter $2$ mm rises steadily through a liquid of density $2000$ kg/m$^3$ at a rate of $0.5$ cm/s, then the coefficient of viscosity of liquid is _______ Poise. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
The terminal velocity of an air bubble rising in a liquid is given by Stokes' law:

$v = \dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^2 \rho g}{\eta}$

Where:
$r = \dfrac{D}{2} = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
$\rho = 2000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
$g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$
$v = 0.5 \text{ cm/s} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$

Rearranging the formula for the coefficient of viscosity $\eta$:

$\eta = \dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^2 \rho g}{v}$

Substituting the given values:

$\eta = \dfrac{2}{9} \times \dfrac{(10^{-3})^2 \times 2000 \times 10}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$

$\eta = \dfrac{2}{9} \times \dfrac{10^{-6} \times 20000}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$

$\eta = \dfrac{2}{9} \times \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$

$\eta = \dfrac{2}{9} \times 4 = \dfrac{8}{9} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$

To convert the viscosity from SI units (Pa$\cdot$s) to CGS units (Poise), we multiply by $10$:

$\eta = \dfrac{8}{9} \times 10 = \dfrac{80}{9} \text{ Poise}$

$\eta \approx 8.88 \text{ Poise}$

The closest given option is $8.8$.

Answer: $8.8$

subject: Physics
q: A air bubble of radius <math><mrow><mn>1</mn><mtext> cm</mtext></mrow></math> in water has an upward acceleration of <math><mrow><mn>9.8</mn><msup><mrow><mtext> cms</mtext></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></math>. The density of water is <math><mrow><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mtext> gm cm</mtext></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></math> and water offers negligible drag force on the bubble. The mass of the bubble is <math><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>980</mn><mo> </mo><mtext>cm</mtext><mo>/</mo><msup><mtext>s</mtext><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></mrow></math>.
question: A air bubble of radius <math><mrow><mn>1</mn><mtext> cm</mtext></mrow></math> in water has an upward acceleration of <math><mrow><mn>9.8</mn><msup><mrow><mtext> cms</mtext></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></math>. The density of water is <math><mrow><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mtext> gm cm</mtext></mrow><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></math> and water offers negligible drag force on the bubble. The mass of the bubble is <math><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>980</mn><mo> </mo><mtext>cm</mtext><mo>/</mo><msup><mtext>s</mtext><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></mrow></math>.
solution:
Fb−mg=ma

Fb=mg+a

=43πr3ρg=mg+a

m=43π1g=m980+9.8

m=4.15

q: Consider a solid sphere of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and mass density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum density of a liquid in which it will float is:
question: Consider a solid sphere of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and mass density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The minimum density of a liquid in which it will float is:
solution:
The density of any point inside the sphere is

ρ=ρ01-r2R2   0<r≤R

weight = buoyant force
mg = B

mg=ρLVg
∫ρ4πr2drg=ρL43πR3g
∫0Rρ01-r2R24πr2dr=ρL43πR3
∫0Rρ04πr2-r4R2dr=ρ04πr33-r55R20R=ρL43πR3
25ρ0=ρL

subject: Physics
q: Two cylindrical vessels of equal cross sectional area of $2 \mathrm{~m}^2$ contain water up to height 10 m and 6 m , respectively. If the vessels are connected at their bottom then the work done by the force of gravity is : (Density of water is $10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
question: Two cylindrical vessels of equal cross sectional area of $2 \mathrm{~m}^2$ contain water up to height 10 m and 6 m , respectively. If the vessels are connected at their bottom then the work done by the force of gravity is : (Density of water is $10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}_1=(\rho \mathrm{A} \times 10) \mathrm{g} \times 5+(\rho \mathrm{A} 6) \mathrm{g} \times 3 \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=\rho \mathrm{Ag}(50+18) \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=68 \rho \mathrm{Ag}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & U_f=(\rho A \times 16) g \times 4 \\ & =(\rho A g) \times 64\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \omega=\Delta U=4 \times \rho A g \\ & =4 \times 1000 \times 2 \times 10=8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A liquid drop of diameter $2$ mm breaks into $512$ droplets. The change in surface energy is $\alpha \times 10^{-6}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Take surface tension of liquid $= 0.08$ N/m)
question: A liquid drop of diameter $2$ mm breaks into $512$ droplets. The change in surface energy is $\alpha \times 10^{-6}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Take surface tension of liquid $= 0.08$ N/m)
solution:
Let the radius of the initial drop be $R$ and the radius of each small droplet be $r$.

Given diameter $D = 2$ mm, so $R = 1$ mm $= 10^{-3}$ m.

By conservation of volume:

$\dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = 512 \times \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$

$R^3 = 512 r^3 \Rightarrow R = 8r \Rightarrow r = \dfrac{R}{8}$

Initial surface area, $A_i = 4\pi R^2$

Final surface area, $A_f = 512 \times 4\pi r^2 = 512 \times 4\pi \left(\dfrac{R}{8}\right)^2 = 32\pi R^2$

Change in surface area, $\Delta A = A_f - A_i = 32\pi R^2 - 4\pi R^2 = 28\pi R^2$

Change in surface energy, $\Delta U = T \Delta A$

$\Delta U = 0.08 \times 28\pi R^2$

Substituting $R = 10^{-3}$ m and $\pi \approx \dfrac{22}{7}$:

$\Delta U = 0.08 \times 28 \times \dfrac{22}{7} \times (10^{-3})^2$

$\Delta U = 0.08 \times 4 \times 22 \times 10^{-6}$

$\Delta U = 7.04 \times 10^{-6}$ J

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-6}$ J, we get $\alpha \approx 7$.

Answer: $7$

subject: Physics
q: Two cylindrical vessels of equal cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> contain water upto heights <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The vessels are interconnected so that the water levels in them become equal. The work done by the force of gravity during the process, is [Take density of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: Two cylindrical vessels of equal cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> contain water upto heights <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The vessels are interconnected so that the water levels in them become equal. The work done by the force of gravity during the process, is [Take density of water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Initially, the centre of mass of the right column will be at height of 75 cm and the centre of mass of the left column will be at 50 cm. After opening of the valve both columns will have height of 125 cm and their centre of mass will be at height 1252 cm. Therefore,

W=-∆U=-m1+m2gh1+h24-m1gh12-m2gh22

Now, m1=ρAh1 and m2=ρAh2

Putting ρ=1000 kg m-3, g=10 m s-2, h1=100 cm, h2=150 cm and A=16×10-4 m2, we get,

W=1 J

subject: Physics
q: A ball is made of a material of density $\rho$ where $\rho_{\text {oil }} < \rho < \rho_{\text {water }}$ with $\rho_{\text {oil }}$ and $\rho_{\text {water }}$ representing the densities of oil and water, respectively. The oil and water are immiscible. If the above ball is in equilibrium in a mixture of this oil and water, which of the following pictures represents its equilibrium position?
question: A ball is made of a material of density $\rho$ where $\rho_{\text {oil }} < \rho < \rho_{\text {water }}$ with $\rho_{\text {oil }}$ and $\rho_{\text {water }}$ representing the densities of oil and water, respectively. The oil and water are immiscible. If the above ball is in equilibrium in a mixture of this oil and water, which of the following pictures represents its equilibrium position?
solution:
$$
\rho_{\text {oil }} < \rho < \rho_{\text {water }}
$$
Oil is the least dense of them so it should settle at the top with water at the base. Now the ball is denser than oil but less denser than water. So, it will sink through oil but will not sink in water. So it will stay at the oil-water interface.

subject: Physics
q:
A spherical ball of radius 1 mm and density 10.5 g cc-1 is dropped in glycerine of coefficient of viscosity 9.8 poise and density 1.5 g cc-1. Viscous force on the ball when it attains constant velocity is 3696×10-x N. The value of x is

(Given, g=9.8 m s-2 and π=227)
question:
A spherical ball of radius 1 mm and density 10.5 g cc-1 is dropped in glycerine of coefficient of viscosity 9.8 poise and density 1.5 g cc-1. Viscous force on the ball when it attains constant velocity is 3696×10-x N. The value of x is

(Given, g=9.8 m s-2 and π=227)
solution:
Viscous force on the ball when it attains constant velocity i.e., terminal velocity is,

 Fv=mg-FB  As, a=0

Here, FB is buoyant force.

⇒Fv=Vρbg-Vρgg=Vgρb-ρg=43π10-33×9.810.5-1.5×103≈3696×10-7 N

Thus, x=7.

subject: Physics
q: A small spherical droplet of density <math><mi>d</mi></math> is floating exactly half immersed in a liquid of density <math><mi>ρ</mi></math> and surface tension <math><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The radius of the droplet is (take note that the surface tension applies an upward force on the droplet):
question: A small spherical droplet of density <math><mi>d</mi></math> is floating exactly half immersed in a liquid of density <math><mi>ρ</mi></math> and surface tension <math><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The radius of the droplet is (take note that the surface tension applies an upward force on the droplet):
solution:
dVg=pV2g+T2πr
⇒d.43πr3g=p.23πr3g+2πrT
⇒23r2g2d-p=2T
⇒r=3T2d-pg

subject: Physics
q:
In the experiment for measurement of viscosity ' $\eta$ ' of given liquid with a ball having radius $R$, consider following statements.
A. Graph between terminal velocity V and R will be a parabola.
B. The terminal velocities of different diameter balls are constant for a given liquid.
C. Measurement of terminal velocity is dependent on the temperature.
D. This experiment can be utilized to assess the density of a given liquid.
E. If balls are dropped with some initial speed, the value of $\eta$ will change.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
In the experiment for measurement of viscosity ' $\eta$ ' of given liquid with a ball having radius $R$, consider following statements.
A. Graph between terminal velocity V and R will be a parabola.
B. The terminal velocities of different diameter balls are constant for a given liquid.
C. Measurement of terminal velocity is dependent on the temperature.
D. This experiment can be utilized to assess the density of a given liquid.
E. If balls are dropped with some initial speed, the value of $\eta$ will change.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The terminal velocity of ball of radius $R$ inside a liquid of viscosity $\eta$ can be written as $V_T=\frac{2 R^2 g}{n}(\sigma-\rho)$, where $\sigma$ is the density of ball and $\delta$ is the density of the liquid.
Hence,
$A$ is correct since $V_T \propto R^2$ gives a parabola on a graph
$C$ is correct since $V_T \alpha \frac{1}{\eta}$ and $\eta$ varies with temperature
$D$ is correct since $V_T \alpha(\sigma-\rho)$ i.e., varies with density of liquid.

subject: Physics
q: The surface tension of soap solution is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The amount of work done required to increase the radius of soap bubble from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> . (take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math>)
question: The surface tension of soap solution is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The amount of work done required to increase the radius of soap bubble from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> . (take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math>)
solution:
The amount of work done is equal to the change in the surface energy to increase the radius of the bubble.

The formula to calculate the change in surface energy is given by

 ΔU=2(T×ΔA)   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required work done.

∆U=3.5×10-2×4π(0.2)2-(0.1)2×2 J=3.5×10-2×4×227×0.03×2=2×1.32×10-2=264×10-4 J

subject: Physics
q: In the diagram shown, the difference in the two tubes of the manometer is 5 cm, the cross-section of the tube at A and B is 6 mm2 and 10 mm2 respectively. The rate at which water flows through the tube is g=10 m s-2
question: In the diagram shown, the difference in the two tubes of the manometer is 5 cm, the cross-section of the tube at A and B is 6 mm2 and 10 mm2 respectively. The rate at which water flows through the tube is g=10 m s-2
solution:
6vA=10vB  ......1

12ρvA2-ρgh=12ρvB2  .........(2)

⇒vA=10.8 m s-1 

 dVdt=AvA

=6×10-2 cm2×1000.8 cm s-1=7.5 cc s-1

OR

By the concept of the venturi meter tube

rate of flow=Q=A1A22g(h1−h2)(A22−A12)  

=6×10×10−42×100×5(−36+100)×10−2

=7.5 cc s-1

subject: Physics
q:
A 400 g solid cube having an edge of length 10 cm floats in water. How much volume of the cube is outside the water ?
(Given : density of water $=1000 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ )
question:
A 400 g solid cube having an edge of length 10 cm floats in water. How much volume of the cube is outside the water ?
(Given : density of water $=1000 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ )
solution:
Volume of cube inside water
$\begin{aligned}
& =\left(\frac{\text { Denslty of cube }}{\text { Denslty of water }}\right) \times \text { Volume of cube } \\
& =\frac{\text { Mass of cube }}{\text { Denslty of water }} \\
& =\frac{400 \mathrm{gm}}{1 \mathrm{gm} / \mathrm{cm}^3} \\
& =400 \mathrm{~cm}^3
\end{aligned}$
Volume of cube outside water
$\begin{aligned}
& =\text { Volume of cube }- \text { Volume of cube inside water } \\
& =1000 \mathrm{~cm}^3-400 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \\
& =600 \mathrm{~cm}^3
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Water flows through a horizontal tube as shown in the figure. The difference in height between the water columns in vertical tubes is 5 cm and the area of cross-sections at $A$ and $B$ are $6 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and $3 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ respectively. The rate of flow will be $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{s}$. (take $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
question: Water flows through a horizontal tube as shown in the figure. The difference in height between the water columns in vertical tubes is 5 cm and the area of cross-sections at $A$ and $B$ are $6 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and $3 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ respectively. The rate of flow will be $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm}^{3} / \mathrm{s}$. (take $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
solution:
Given: $h = 5$ cm, $A_1 = 6$ cm², $A_2 = 3$ cm², $g = 1000$ cm/s²
By continuity: $A_1v_1 = A_2v_2 \Rightarrow v_2 = 2v_1$
By Bernoulli's equation: $P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$
Since $P_1 - P_2 = \rho gh$:
$\rho gh = \frac{1}{2}\rho(4v_1^2 - v_1^2) = \frac{3\rho v_1^2}{2}$
$v_1 = \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1000 \times 5}{3}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt{3}}$ cm/s
Rate of flow $Q = A_1v_1 = 6 \times \frac{100}{\sqrt{3}} = 200\sqrt{3}$ cm³/s

subject: Physics
q:
A tub is filled with water and a wooden cube $10$ cm $\times$ $10$ cm $\times$ $10$ cm is placed in the water. The wooden cube is found to float on the water with a part of it submerged in water. When a metal coin is placed on the wooden cube, the submerged part is increased by $3.87$ cm. The mass of the metal coin is _______ gram.
(Take water density as $1$ g/cm$^3$ and density of wood as $0.4$ g/cm$^3$)
question:
A tub is filled with water and a wooden cube $10$ cm $\times$ $10$ cm $\times$ $10$ cm is placed in the water. The wooden cube is found to float on the water with a part of it submerged in water. When a metal coin is placed on the wooden cube, the submerged part is increased by $3.87$ cm. The mass of the metal coin is _______ gram.
(Take water density as $1$ g/cm$^3$ and density of wood as $0.4$ g/cm$^3$)
solution:
Let $A$ be the base area of the wooden cube.

$A = 10 \text{ cm} \times 10 \text{ cm} = 100 \text{ cm}^2$

Let $\Delta x$ be the increase in the submerged depth of the cube when the coin is placed on it.

$\Delta x = 3.87 \text{ cm}$

The additional volume of water displaced due to the weight of the metal coin is $\Delta V = A \times \Delta x$.

$\Delta V = 100 \times 3.87 = 387 \text{ cm}^3$

By Archimedes' principle, the weight of the metal coin is balanced by the additional buoyant force, which is equal to the weight of the additional water displaced.

$m g = \Delta V \rho_w g$

$m = \Delta V \rho_w$

Substituting the values of $\Delta V$ and the density of water $\rho_w = 1 \text{ g/cm}^3$:

$m = 387 \text{ cm}^3 \times 1 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 387 \text{ g}$

Answer: $387$

subject: Physics
q: A big drop is formed by coalescing 1000 small droplets of water. The ratio of surface energy of 1000 droplets to that of energy of big drop is $\frac{10}{x}$. The value of $x$ is __________
question: A big drop is formed by coalescing 1000 small droplets of water. The ratio of surface energy of 1000 droplets to that of energy of big drop is $\frac{10}{x}$. The value of $x$ is __________
solution: $\begin{aligned} 1000 \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 & =\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \\ 10 r & =R \\ R & =10 r\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} \frac{\text { S.E. of } 1000 \text { drops }}{\text { S.E. of Big drop }} & =\frac{1000\left(4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2\right) \mathrm{T}}{4 \pi \mathrm{R}^2 \mathrm{~T}} \\ & =\frac{1000 \times \mathrm{r}^2}{(10 \mathrm{r})^2}=10=\frac{10}{\mathrm{x}} \\ \therefore \quad x & =1\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A thin uniform tube is bent into a circle of radius r in the vertical plane. Equal volumes of two immiscible liquids, Whose densities are <math><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>(</mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> fill half the circle. The angle <math><mi>θ</mi></math> between the radius vector passing through the common interface and the vertical is:
question: A thin uniform tube is bent into a circle of radius r in the vertical plane. Equal volumes of two immiscible liquids, Whose densities are <math><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>(</mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mrow><mi>ρ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> fill half the circle. The angle <math><mi>θ</mi></math> between the radius vector passing through the common interface and the vertical is:
solution:
Pressure at the point A is the same on both sides of the tube, 
ρ 1 gr(cosθ−sinθ)= ρ 2 gr(sinθ+cosθ)
⇒ρ1ρ2=sinθ+cosθcosθ-sinθ=tanθ+11-tanθ
⇒ρ1-ρ1tanθ=ρ2+ρ2tanθ
⇒ρ1+ρ2tanθ=ρ1-ρ2

θ=tan-1⁡ρ1-ρ2ρ1+ρ2

subject: Physics
q: The reading of pressure metre attached with a closed pipe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. On opening the valve, water starts flowing and the reading of pressure metre falls to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The velocity of water is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>V</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is _________.
question: The reading of pressure metre attached with a closed pipe is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. On opening the valve, water starts flowing and the reading of pressure metre falls to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The velocity of water is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>V</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is _________.
solution:
The formula to calculate the change in pressure can be written as

P1-P2=12ρv2   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

4.5×104-2.0×104=12×103×v2⇒2.5×104=12×103×v2⇒v2=50⇒v=50

Hence, V=50.

subject: Physics
q: A large number of droplets, each of radius, $r$ coalesce to form a bigger drop of radius, $R$. An engineer designs a machine so that the energy released in this process is converted into the kinetic energy of the drop. Velocity of the drop is ( $T=$ surface tension, $\rho=$ density)
question: A large number of droplets, each of radius, $r$ coalesce to form a bigger drop of radius, $R$. An engineer designs a machine so that the energy released in this process is converted into the kinetic energy of the drop. Velocity of the drop is ( $T=$ surface tension, $\rho=$ density)
solution:
When small droplets coelesce to form a bigger drop, energy released in this process is given by,
$$
4 \pi R^3 T\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right]
$$
Where, $R=$ radius of big drop
$r=$ radius of small drop
$T=$ surface tension
According to question
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{2} m v^2=4 \pi R^3 T\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right] \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \rho\right] v^2=4 \pi R^3 T\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right] \\
\Rightarrow V^2 & =\frac{6 T}{\rho}\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right] \Rightarrow V=\left[\frac{6 T}{\rho}\left(\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{R}\right)\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The top of a water tank is open to air and its water level is maintained. It is giving out <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.74</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> water per minute through a circular opening of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> radius in its wall. The depth of the centre of the opening from the level of water in the tank is close to:
question: The top of a water tank is open to air and its water level is maintained. It is giving out <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.74</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> water per minute through a circular opening of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> radius in its wall. The depth of the centre of the opening from the level of water in the tank is close to:
solution:
Volume of water flowing out=Av, where A is area of opening and v is speed of water from the opening.

Volume=πr2v

0.74  m3min=3.14×2×10-22×2×10×H m3s×60 smin

⇒H=0.7460×3.14×4×10-42×120

⇒H=4.8 m

Hence, the height of water in the tank was 4.8 m from the bottom of the tank.

subject: Physics
q: Consider a water tank as shown in the figure. It's cross-sectional area is 0.4 m2. The tank has an opening B near the bottom whose cross-section area is 1 cm2. A load of 24 kg is applied on the water at the top when the height of the water level is 40 cm above the bottom, the velocity of water coming out the opening B is v m s-1. The value of v, to the nearest integer, is ___ .[Take the value of g to be 10 m s-2]
question: Consider a water tank as shown in the figure. It's cross-sectional area is 0.4 m2. The tank has an opening B near the bottom whose cross-section area is 1 cm2. A load of 24 kg is applied on the water at the top when the height of the water level is 40 cm above the bottom, the velocity of water coming out the opening B is v m s-1. The value of v, to the nearest integer, is ___ .[Take the value of g to be 10 m s-2]
solution:
m=24 kg

A=0.4 m2

a=1 cm2

H=40 cm

Using Bernoulli's equation, 

⇒P0+mgA+ρgH+12Pv12

=P0+0+12ρv2   ...1

⇒ Neglecting v1

⇒v=2gH+2mgAρ

⇒v=8+1.2

⇒v=3.033 m s-1

⇒v≃3 m s-1

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement- 2 . Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statetnents.
Statement-1: A capillary is dipped in a liquid and liquid rises to a height $h$ in it. As the temperature of the liquid is raised, the height $h$ increases (if the density of the liquid and the angle of contact remain the same).
Statement-2: Surface tension of a liquid decreases with the rise in its temperature.
question:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement- 2 . Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statetnents.
Statement-1: A capillary is dipped in a liquid and liquid rises to a height $h$ in it. As the temperature of the liquid is raised, the height $h$ increases (if the density of the liquid and the angle of contact remain the same).
Statement-2: Surface tension of a liquid decreases with the rise in its temperature.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not the correct explanation for Statement-1.
Statement-1 is false, Statement- 2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement- 2 is false.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation for Statement-1.
solution:
Surface tension of a liquid decreases with the rise in temperture. At the boiling point of liquid, surface tension is zero.
Capillary rise $h=\frac{2 \mathrm{~T} \cos \theta}{\mathrm{rdg}}$
As surface tension $\mathrm{T}$ decreases with rise in temperature hence capillary rise also decreases.

subject: Physics
q:
An open glass tube is immersed in mercury in such a way that a length of 8 cm extends above the mercury level. The open end of the tube is then closed and sealed and the tube is raised vertically up by additional 46 cm. What will be length of the air column above mercury in the tube now ?
(Atmospheric pressure = 76 cm of Hg)
question:
An open glass tube is immersed in mercury in such a way that a length of 8 cm extends above the mercury level. The open end of the tube is then closed and sealed and the tube is raised vertically up by additional 46 cm. What will be length of the air column above mercury in the tube now ?
(Atmospheric pressure = 76 cm of Hg)
solution:
Using isothermal process conditions



⇒ P 1 V 1 = P 2 V 2 ⇒ P 1 A ℓ 1 = P 2 A ℓ 2

8 p 0 = xp ⇒ p = 8 p 0 x     (here p0 = 76 cm of Hg)

8 p 0 x + ρ m g 54 - x = ρ m g (76)

8 x + 54 - x 76 = 1

x = 16 cm

subject: Physics
q: A small ball of mass $m$ and density $\rho$ is dropped in a viscous liquid of density $\rho_0$. After sometime, the ball falls with constant velocity. The viscous force on the ball is :
question: A small ball of mass $m$ and density $\rho$ is dropped in a viscous liquid of density $\rho_0$. After sometime, the ball falls with constant velocity. The viscous force on the ball is :
solution:
$\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}-\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{ma}$
$\mathrm{a}=0$ for constant velocity
$\begin{aligned}
& m g-F_B=F_v \\
& F_v=m g-v \rho_0 g=m g-\frac{m}{\rho} \rho_0 g=m g\left(1-\frac{\rho_0}{\rho}\right)
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The average mass of rain drops is $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~kg}$ and their avarage terminal velocity is $9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Calculate the energy transferred by rain to each square metre of the surface at a place which receives $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ of rain in a year.
question: The average mass of rain drops is $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~kg}$ and their avarage terminal velocity is $9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Calculate the energy transferred by rain to each square metre of the surface at a place which receives $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ of rain in a year.
solution:
Total volume of rain drops, received $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ in a year by area $1 \mathrm{~m}^2$
$$
=1 \mathrm{~m}^2 \times \frac{100}{100} \mathrm{~m}=1 \mathrm{~m}^3
$$
As we know, density of water,
$$
d=10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3
$$
Therefore, mass of this volume of water $M=d \times v=10^3 \times 1=10^3 \mathrm{~kg}$
Average terminal velocity of rain drop $v=9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ (given)
Therefore, energy transferred by rain,
$$
\begin{aligned}
&E=\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \\
&=\frac{1}{2} \times 10^3 \times(9)^2 \\
&=\frac{1}{2} \times 10^3 \times 81=4.05 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Small water droplets of radius $0.01 \mathrm{~mm}$ are formed in the upper atmosphere and falling with a terminal velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. Due to condensation, if 8 such droplets are coalesced and formed a larger drop, the new terminal velocity will be _____$\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: Small water droplets of radius $0.01 \mathrm{~mm}$ are formed in the upper atmosphere and falling with a terminal velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. Due to condensation, if 8 such droplets are coalesced and formed a larger drop, the new terminal velocity will be _____$\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
$\mathrm{m}=$ mass of small drop
$\mathrm{M}=$ mass of bigger drop
$\begin{aligned} & V_t=\frac{2}{9} \frac{R^2(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta} \\ & 8 \propto m=M \\ & 8 r^3=R^3 \Rightarrow R=2 R\end{aligned}$
as $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{t}} \times \mathrm{R}^2 \quad \because$ Radius double so $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{t}}$ becomes 4 time
$\therefore 4 \times 10=40 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q: If the terminal speed of a sphere of gold (density $=19.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ) is $0.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in a viscous liquid (density $=1.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ) of the same size in the same liquid.
question: If the terminal speed of a sphere of gold (density $=19.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ) is $0.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in a viscous liquid (density $=1.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ ) of the same size in the same liquid.
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{g}}}=\frac{\left(\rho_{\mathrm{s}}-\rho_{\ell}\right)}{\left(\rho_{\mathrm{g}}-\rho_{\ell}\right)}$
$\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{s}}=0.1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q: A soap bubble is blown to a diameter of $7 \mathrm{~cm} .36960 \mathrm{erg}$ of work is done in blowing it further. If surface tension of soap solution is $40 \mathrm{dyne} / \mathrm{cm}$ then the new radius is______ $\mathrm{cm}$ Take $\left(\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right)$
question: A soap bubble is blown to a diameter of $7 \mathrm{~cm} .36960 \mathrm{erg}$ of work is done in blowing it further. If surface tension of soap solution is $40 \mathrm{dyne} / \mathrm{cm}$ then the new radius is______ $\mathrm{cm}$ Take $\left(\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \omega=\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{S} \Delta \mathrm{A} \\ & 36960 \mathrm{erg}=\frac{40 \text { dyne }}{\mathrm{cm}} 8 \pi\left[(\mathrm{r})^2-\left(\frac{7}{2}\right)^2\right] \mathrm{cm}^2 \\ & \mathrm{r}=7 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Wax is coated on the inner wall of a capillary tube and the tube is then dipped in water. Then, compared to the unwaxed capillary, the angle of contact $\theta$ and the height $h$ upto which water rises change. These changes are :
question: Wax is coated on the inner wall of a capillary tube and the tube is then dipped in water. Then, compared to the unwaxed capillary, the angle of contact $\theta$ and the height $h$ upto which water rises change. These changes are :
solution:
Angle of $\operatorname{contact} \theta$
$$
\cos \theta=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SA}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SL}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{LA}}}
$$
when water is on a waxy or oily surface
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SA}} < \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SL}} \cos \theta$ is negative i.e.,
$$
90^{\circ} < \theta < 180^{\circ}
$$
i.e., angle of contact $\theta$ increases
And for $\theta>90^{\circ}$ liquid level in capillary
tube fall. i.e., h decreases
